title: AI Threat Intel Inside National Policy

modules.title: Why Siloed Threat Data Became a National Security Liability
modules.sub: Tracing how fragmented intel creates exploitable gaps for adversaries
modules.description: Learners examine historical and contemporary cases where siloed threat data allowed nation-state actors to operate undetected across organizational and jurisdictional boundaries. They analyze the structural, legal, and cultural incentives that sustain silos and assess the cumulative risk those silos create at a national level.

modules.title: How AI Accelerates Threat Intelligence Analysis and Sharing
modules.sub: Mapping AI capabilities that compress detection and dissemination timelines
modules.description: Learners survey the specific AI and machine learning techniques—NLP, graph analytics, automated indicator enrichment—that reduce the time from raw data collection to actionable threat intelligence. They evaluate where AI genuinely shortens the kill chain and where human judgment remains irreplaceable in the sharing pipeline.

modules.title: Legal and Sovereignty Constraints on Cross-Border AI Intel
modules.sub: Navigating data residency, privacy law, and jurisdictional boundaries in practice
modules.description: Learners work through the key legal frameworks—GDPR, CLOUD Act, national data-localization statutes—that constrain what threat data can cross borders and how AI can process it. They identify the specific points in a cross-border sharing workflow where compliance friction is highest and map mitigation strategies for each.

modules.title: Frameworks in Practice: CISA, MITRE ATT&CK, and EU AI Act Intersections
modules.sub: Aligning operational threat frameworks with emerging AI governance requirements
modules.description: Learners compare the operational expectations of CISA's sharing directives and MITRE ATT&CK's structured taxonomy against the risk-classification and transparency requirements introduced by the EU AI Act. They practice mapping a realistic sharing scenario across all three frameworks simultaneously to surface conflicts and compliant pathways.

modules.title: Information Sharing Agreements: What AI Can and Cannot Automate
modules.sub: Defining the automation boundary inside ISACs, ISAOs, and bilateral agreements
modules.description: Learners dissect the clauses in common information sharing agreements—ISAC membership terms, bilateral MOUs, STIX/TAXII data use policies—to determine which steps AI can execute autonomously and which require human authorization. They produce a decision matrix that an organization can use to scope AI automation within its existing agreement portfolio.

modules.title: Case Study: State-Sponsored AI Campaigns and the Policy Response Gap
modules.sub: Diagnosing where policy lagged behind AI-enabled adversary operations
modules.description: Learners analyze documented state-sponsored campaigns in which AI-assisted reconnaissance, phishing, or malware development outpaced the policy and coordination mechanisms designed to counter them. They identify the specific decision points where faster or broader threat sharing could have altered the outcome and evaluate what policy changes were—or were not—enacted afterward.

modules.title: Designing Compliant AI-Augmented Threat Sharing Programs
modules.sub: Building a sharing program architecture that satisfies policy and performs operationally
modules.description: Learners apply the course's frameworks, legal constraints, and automation boundaries to design an end-to-end AI-augmented threat sharing program for a defined organizational context. They produce a program blueprint that specifies data flows, AI tool roles, human review gates, and the compliance checkpoints required by relevant national and cross-border policy frameworks.

synopsis: Nation-state actors are using AI to accelerate cyberattacks while defenders remain hamstrung by legal, political, and technical barriers to sharing the threat intelligence that could stop them. This course maps the policy landscape, shows where AI can legally and practically accelerate sharing, and prepares professionals to operate inside real compliance constraints.
rationale: As governments race to legislate AI use in critical infrastructure, security professionals have almost no structured guidance on where AI-augmented threat sharing is legally permissible and operationally effective.
learning_outcome: Learners can assess their organization's threat intelligence sharing posture against current national policy frameworks and identify specific AI-augmented sharing workflows that remain legally compliant.
source_signals: Trump AI plan calls for cybersecurity assessments, threat info-sharing
Microsoft Releases 2025 Digital Defense Report: Highlighting the Changing Cyber Threat Landscape and the Importance of Security in the AI Era
